original_abstract: Precise control over the excited-state dynamics of first-row transition metal complexes is a long-standing challenge in inorganic photochemistry. While $d^6$ complexes based on precious metals like Ru(II), Os(II), and Ir(III) are cornerstones of modern photocatalysis and solar energy conversion, their earth-abundant 3$d^6$ counterparts typically suffer from ultrafast non-radiative deactivation via low-lying metal-centered states. Here, we report the development of Cr(0) tris(diisocyanide) complexes that overcome these limitations through targeted molecular design. By employing chelate ligands that provide strong ligand fields and structural rigidity, we achieved metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) excited-state lifetimes of up to 47 ns and photoluminescence quantum yields exceeding 1% in solution at room temperature. These photophysical properties are directly competitive with benchmark Os(II) complexes. Leveraging their exceptionally high reducing power in the excited state ($E_{ox}^* \approx -2.7$ V vs. $\text{Fc}^{+/0}$), we demonstrate that these Cr(0) luminophores can drive demanding photoredox transformations, including the reductive dehalogenation of aryl halides and overall redox-neutral C–C coupling reactions under red-light irradiation. This work establishes a viable strategy for replacing noble metals with abundant first-row transition metals in MLCT-based photochemical applications.
humanized_abstract: Control over the photochemical excited state behavior of first-row transition metal complexes has long been an area of challenge and interest in inorganic photochemistry. While many $d^6$ precious metal complexes such as Ru(II), Os(II) and Ir(III) derivatives have been heavily utilized in recent years in applications such as photocatalysis and solar energy conversion, their earth abundant 3$d$ counterparts are more often than not limited by their ultrafast non-radiative quenching through short-lived metal centred states. Here we report that by employing chelate ligands with high ligand field energies and a high degree of structural constraint about the metal centre, we can create Cr(0) derivatives of tris(diisocyanide) character which exhibit metal to ligand charge transfer excited states with lifetimes of up to 47 ns and with photoluminescence quantum yields of over 1% in solution at room temperature. These values are comparable to the best reported Os(II) derivatives. Utilizing the high oxidising power of the excited state (Eox∗ ≈ -2.7 V vs Fc+/0) derived from these Cr(0) luminophores, we are able to act as catalysts in demanding photoredox processes which include the reduction of aryl halides and overall redox neutral coupling reactions between different organic fragments achieved under red-light illumination. In summary, we show that it is possible to create 3$d^6$ first row transition metal photochemical catalysts that are competitive to their precious metal analogues through judicious molecular design.

While many $d^6$ precious metal complexes such as Ru(II), Os(II) and Ir(III) derivatives have been heavily utilized in recent years in applications such as photocatalysis and solar energy conversion, their earth abundant 3$d$ counterparts are more often than not limited by their ultrafast non-radiative quenching through short-lived metal centred states. Here we report that by employing chelate ligands with high ligand field energies and a high degree of structural constraint about the metal centre, we can create Cr(0) derivatives of tris(diisocyanide) character which exhibit metal to ligand charge transfer excited states with lifetimes of up to 47 ns and with photoluminescence quantum yields of over 1% in solution at room temperature. These values are comparable to the best reported Os(II) derivatives. Utilizing the high oxidising power of the excited state (Eox∗ ≈ -2.7 V vs Fc+/0) derived from these Cr(0) luminophores, we are able to act as catalysts in demanding photoredox processes which include the reduction of aryl halides and overall redox neutral coupling reactions between different organic fragments achieved under red-light illumination. In summary, we show that it is possible to create 3$d^6$ first row transition metal photochemical catalysts that are competitive to their precious metal analogues through judicious molecular design.
